text: One of the most ubiquitous effects of behavior is the speed-accuracy tradeoff (SAT). From insects to rodents to primates, faster decisions are almost invariably accompanied by worse accuracy and slower decisions by better accuracy. Recent advances in neuroscience methods are starting to uncover the neuronal components of the SAT, while computational models are being forced to confront this ubiquitous behavioral effect. Here I summarize the effects, history and influence of the SAT, as well as providing a "users guide" for experimental and analytical methods for studying it. I also review the behavior that results from directly manipulating components of the SAT, and finally I review some of the many applications of this method.
explanation: The text presents a coherent, human-like scholarly abstract with a clear first-person perspective and a structured overview typical of a literature review.
